g.data-narration: Welcome to the comprehensive lesson on Hydrogen as an Alternative Energy
g.text: Comprehensive Visual Guide for Engineering Students

g.data-narration: Hydrogen energy uses hydrogen as an energy carrier - it must be produced from other sources then stored and converted to electricity
g.text: Hydrogen is an energy CARRIER (not a primary source) - must be produced from water, natural gas, or biomass.
When used, it produces only water vapor - zero harmful emissions at point of use!

g.data-narration: Hydrogen has the highest energy content by weight - 120 MJ/kg, which is 3x higher than gasoline
Hydrogen is produced through four main methods: steam methane reforming, electrolysis, biomass gasification, and photoelectrochemical splitting
g.text: Hydrogen must be extracted from compounds - it's not found freely in nature

g.data-narration: Steam methane reforming uses natural gas and steam at high temperatures to produce hydrogen - this is the most common method but releases CO2
g.text: Most common method - accounts for ~95% of global production

g.data-narration: Electrolysis splits water into hydrogen and oxygen using electricity - produces green hydrogen when powered by renewables
g.text: Water is split into hydrogen and oxygen using electrical energy

g.data-narration: Hydrogen can be stored as compressed gas at 350-700 bar, as liquid at -253C, or in solid metal hydrides
Fuel cells generate electricity through electrochemical reaction - hydrogen splits at anode, protons pass through electrolyte, electrons create current
g.text: Electrochemical conversion: Chemical energy → Electrical energy + Water + Heat

g.data-narration: There are three main fuel cell types - PEMFC for vehicles, SOFC for stationary power, and AFC for aerospace applications
Hydrogen has diverse applications in transportation (cars, buses, trains), power generation, and industrial processes like steel manufacturing
Hydrogen energy offers zero emissions at use, high efficiency of 50-60%, energy storage capability, and reduced energy dependency
Key challenges include high production costs especially for green hydrogen, storage difficulties, and lack of infrastructure
Hydrogen contains 120 MJ/kg - 1 kg produces 33 kWh, and fuel cells achieve 55% efficiency for 18 kWh electrical output
g.text: Calculate energy from 1 kg hydrogen in combustion vs fuel cell
⚡ Fuel cells get ~3x more range than batteries per kg of hydrogen

g.data-narration: Electrolysis of 10 liters water produces 1.11 kg hydrogen occupying 12.4 cubic meters at standard conditions
g.text: How much hydrogen from 10L water? What volume at STP?

g.data-narration: Steam methane reforming produces 22 kg CO2 per kg hydrogen while solar electrolysis produces only 2-5 kg - a 77-91% reduction
Remember these key formulas: electrolysis produces hydrogen and oxygen, SMR uses methane and steam, fuel cells combine hydrogen and oxygen
In summary: hydrogen is a versatile clean energy carrier, green hydrogen via electrolysis is the sustainable path, and it enables zero-emission transportation and energy storage
g.text: • Hydrogen is an ENERGY CARRIER - must be produced from other sources
• Zero emissions at use, 50-60% fuel cell efficiency
• Challenges: Cost, storage, infrastructure - but rapidly improving!
